>Hmm. There's a lot about the pep8 style that I don't like, so I'm afraid I have issues with a lot of the style changes you made here. 

Right.  I have my own ignore list for flake8 too.  It just looks a bit different than yours.  For the record, here's my list:

- C901 function is too complex
- E125 continuation line with same indent as next logical line
- E129 visually indented line with same indent as next logical line
- E226 missing whitespace around arithmetic operator
- E261 at least two spaces before inline comment
- E402 module level import not at top of file
- E731 do not assign a lambda expression, use a def

and here's the list you're proposing to add:

- E116 unexpected indentation (comment)
- E126 continuation line over-indented for hanging indent
- E201 whitespace after '('
- E202 whitespace before ')'
- E228 missing whitespace around modulo operator
- E231 missing whitespace after ‘,’
- E241 multiple spaces after ‘,’
- E701 multiple statements on one line (colon)

I’ll note that 126 and 241 are “standard”, and I think a few of your objections are probably special cases and not general rules.

The remaining guidelines I think I address most often in GalSim are:

- E251 unexpected spaces around keyword / parameter equals
- E301 expected 1 blank line, found 0
- E302 expected 2 blank lines, found 0
- E303 too many blank lines

Mostly, I’d just like to have the style more consistent throughout GalSim, but I’m not overly attached to what that style should be.

Good.  I made a few more algorithmic tweaks.  (Basically the ones I suggested earlier.)  And the last test is now down to 1.7 seconds on my laptop.  Which means that the accumulate function is only taking about 1/2 the time now.  (Shooting the photons in the first place is most of the rest of the time.)  So I'm pretty happy with that now.

I might have messed up your PhotonArray writing option.  I moved it to the PhotonArray class (`photons.write(file_name)`) and have it write out to a fits file rather than ascii both for speed and to avoid loss of precision.  The file can be read back into a PhotonArray object with `photons = galsim.PhotonArray.read(file_name)`.

But if this messes up your ability to read it back into your code, let me know, and I can add an ASCII option for the write function to output in the format you had before.

> I also did not understand the indexing. If obj_num is always increasing and the main object is built last, then how is k = config['obj_num'] - config['blend_first'] greater than zero?

'obj_num' increases with each stamp.  Normally, that means each object, but in this case, several objects are build using the same `obj_num` index.  

We build all the objects in the blend when we are at the first stamp in the set.  So when 'obj_num == blend_first', we used that random number generator to build all the objects in the blend.  After that, for the next n_neighbors stamps, we don't regenerate any profiles, since they are all built already.  We just grab a different cutout from the cached image.

> On 06 Jul 2016, at 02:13, Dr Nic Williams <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> 
> From v1.0 release notes:
> 
> When upgrading from v0.90 or below, always upgrade all replicas before the master. Since we don't store replication credentials in DCS anymore, an old replica won't be able to connect to the new master.
> 
> Is this requirement just for upgrading to v1.0 because of change in where replicas get credentials for leader? Or will this "replicas first, leader last" requirement be for all future updates too?
> 

Generally, this is a reasonable thing to do on any upgrade, since you don’t want an upgrade-related accident to kill your master, and rather have it first tried on the replicas. That being said, the notice above is related specifically to the change of what Patroni stores in  DCS, so it’s very likely that the future upgrades will not require doing things in this particular order.

Previously, #1328 simply skipped the sorting option, since it complicates things a little bit. But, indeed, this will be a good option.

To implement sorting, I think there are two options:
1. The Heap-based (adopted from tensorflow) algorithm already returns sorted tensor. But again this algorithm is not suited for large `k` values.
2. For full sorting, we may need specific SortingKernel, here are some references:
From tensorflow: [LaunchSortKernel](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/eeab2c867faa0f10dfea8635d1e87009844f902e/tensorflow/core/kernels/topk_op_gpu.cu.cc#L430)
From pytorch: [sortKeyValueInplace](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/0859f0e3e6ceb02f56551271476009ad7300e697/aten/src/THC/generic/THCTensorSort.cu#L8) =>[bitonicSortKVInPlace](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/502aaf39cf4a878f9e4f849e5f409573aa598aa9/aten/src/THC/THCSortUtils.cuh#L139) or [sortViaThrust](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/0859f0e3e6ceb02f56551271476009ad7300e697/aten/src/THC/generic/THCTensorSort.cu#L156)
I think these can be adapted, but modifications are needed. 

I wonder if there are any other easy-to-use&adapt GPU-Sorting Kernel.

>>> class C(object):
...     def foo(self):
...             pass
... 
>>> def bar(arg): pass
... 
>>> with MonkeyPatch('__main__.C.foo', bar):
...     C().foo()
... 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 2, in <module>
TypeError: bar() takes exactly 1 argument (0 given)
>>> C._bar = bar
>>> with MonkeyPatch('__main__.C.foo', C._bar):
...     C().foo()
... 
>>> 

This illustrates the failure, and a workaround for Python2. Assigning to C._bar ends up being functionally equivalent to defining it on C. The reason for the failure is that the first run of _setattr() ends up looking at bar when deciding if it should wrap the function with staticmethod(). So the first part of the issue is that _setattr looks at the right function when called for addCleanup, but not when doing to original patch override.


The other part of the failure comes from a situation like the following:

>>> class TestSomething(object):
...     def __init__(self):
...             self._foo = C.foo
...     def fake_thing(self):
...             self._foo()
...     def test_that_thing(self):
...             with MonkeyPatch('__main__.C.foo', self.fake_thing):
...                     C().foo()
... 
>>> TestSomething().test_that_thing()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<stdin>", line 8, in test_that_thing
  File "<stdin>", line 5, in fake_thing
TypeError: unbound method foo() must be called with C instance as first argument (got nothing instead)

Here was one attempt to work around it, with fake_thing fleshed out to be more indicative of why something like this might be done.

>>> class TestSomething(object):
...     def __init__(self):
...             self._foo = C.foo
...     def fake_thing(self):
...             value = self._foo(self)
...             value.sort()
...             return value
...     def test_that_thing(self):
...             with MonkeyPatch('__main__.C.foo', self.fake_thing):
...                     C().foo()
... 
>>> TestSomething().test_that_thing()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<stdin>", line 10, in test_that_thing
  File "<stdin>", line 5, in fake_thing
TypeError: unbound method foo() must be called with C instance as first argument (got TestSomething instance instead)

So I need to amend the commit message, and bug report as I missed a detail of this originally. It's not that the method used for patching needs to be defined on C, or assigned to C as if it were defined there, it's that if the patching method references methods on C through self then it needs to be defined on C.
This fixes both sides of it now, patching the method and cleaning up the patch. And all cases are tested.

- [How we remove all created resources from the `before` and `after` hooks](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-node/blob/7b3042afc2185aa14d461b76bc7b644b75eb1b6c/packages/compute/system-test/compute.js#L1408). It will get, then delete each resource type using the filter: `'name eq gcloud-tests.*'`.

`klepto` doesn't have a notion of a no longer "valid" entry.  What kind of things would make an entry no longer valid…?  Cache entry expires after a certain amount of time…?

Your function has three parts:
1. lookup: handled by `function.lookup(*args,**kwds)`
2. delete: handled by `cache.pop(function.key(*args, **kwds))` and similarly with `cache.archive.pop`
3. validation: not currently handled

With regard to (2), it's potentially a good idea to have some flavor of a `pop-thru` function, which would pop from both cache and archive.  (1) is simple as is.  For (3), I'm not sure what the compelling use cases are yet, so I'm not sure what's best to do in that regard.  

If you are worried about there being a `key` corresponding to a file (as is in the case of a `dir_archive`) where the file no longer exists… that shouldn't ever be the case as the archive is always built from inspecting the list of files on disk.  Then, if you have an entry in `cache` that is not in `archive`, because the file was deleted… you can use `dump`, `load`, or `sync` to update either the cache or archive as desired.  Maybe a more compelling case is if the file becomes corrupt for some reason… and needs to be dropped, but then you'd just `del` or `pop` the file from the archive.

So maybe my question is why should `valid` be a cache method, or some free-standing function?

There is nothing stopping you from using the `troposphere.Tags` in any of the above files, even though they require a `list`. `troposphere.Tags` outputs a list so it's definitely usable, but the opposite is not true. You can't supply a list when the Tags parameter is requiring the `troposphere.Tags` class.

So, I don't really see a way to change the above files to require `troposphere.Tags` without breaking all previous templates that simply supply a list. We _could_ do the opposite and change all files that require `troposphere.Tags` to require just a `list` instead, but that doesn't seem productive either.

So I guess the way I'm understanding it, we have 3 options.

1) Leave it the way it is
2) Introduce a breaking change to the `Tags` parameter for some files in a Major version bump (like version 2.0.0)
3) Convert all Tags parameters to require a `list` instead of requiring `troposphere.Tags`

> "Is pex meant to be used for development, or only deployment?"

in general, I'd like to think we're pretty agnostic on the "purpose" of pex. afaict there's no canonical workflow that pex is better suited for - it's just a tool and library that you can compose into your development, deployment or other such process.

for the most part in practice tho, we (at Twitter) use `pex` as a library via `pants` for development - but that's largely because all of our other development work uses `pants` as well and this helps us do things like share thrift libraries etc between jvm/python apps. how to use pants w/ pex in your development process is already covered in the pants docs (starting here: http://pantsbuild.github.io/python_readme.html and scattered throughout). all of the python related functionality in pants (`python_binary`, `python_library` + the `binary`, `test` goals and so on) utilize pex under the hood via its API. to that end, the cmd workflow you described essentially mirrors how pants' uses pex at a high level, except without the explicit PYTHONPATH manipulation and with pants-based caching/fingerprinting.

I personally tend to use `pex(1)` quite a bit in addition to `pants` for general purpose "development" use - trying out libraries/tools (and at specific target versions) without letting them mutate my installation environment, testing resolves, debugging, etc. I haven't had to (explicitly) create a virtualenv in years and I'm pretty happy about that.

so with that said, I'm not sure this sort of guidance is something pex in general is opinionated enough about to formally document.

I was able to add minimumCompressionSize by including it as such in my swagger DefinitionBody:

```
"DefinitionBody": {
          "swagger": "2.0",
          "info": { "title": { "Ref": "AWS::StackName" } },
          "x-amazon-apigateway-minimum-compression-size": 1024
...
}
```

The other two fields,

`requireAuthorizationForCacheControl`

and

`UnauthorizedCacheControlHeaderStrategy`

seem to default to the values I have listed, however I would still prefer if all of these things were configurable without a custom resource.
You cannot use the shorthand notation in SAM. Please use, e.g., `Fn::Join` and `Ref` instead. See the discussion above in this very issue.

At the very least, it'd be good to add it to the list of properties for `AWS::Serverless::Api`. If (when) we get support for multiple stages, that could be set on a per-stage level to handle use cases where e.g. we have several environments (currently, we just keep them in separate stacks, which is fine).

> I will comment on that PR shortly, but the short version of the reply is that I find that the parallel implementation in the same module doesn't do anything good for readability of an already complicated module and I do not like the idea of pulling twisted in as a general dependency, so I would not favor that PR.

That would allow us to keep the simpler implementation in both modules and make the twisted dependency optional, by catching import errors and formatting an easy to understand exception that twisted will need to be installed separately. The downside to this is of course that someone will need to take ownership of this module and port improvements.

>
> First, the pull request does not make twisted a dependency of soco (just as the approach you advocate also doesn't do this). ```
import time
import soco
from soco.snapshot import Snapshot

class SonosInterface(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.zones = None
        while not self.zones:
            self.zones = soco.discover(timeout=3)

    def doorbell(self):
        for zone in self.zones:
            zone.snap = Snapshot(zone)
            zone.snap.snapshot()
            print('found zone')
            print(zone.snap.volume, zone.is_coordinator, zone.is_playing_tv,  zone.player_name)

        coordinators = [zone for zone in self.zones if zone.is_coordinator and not zone.is_playing_tv]

        print coordinators

        for zone_c in coordinators:
            trans_state = zone_c.get_current_transport_info()
            print(zone_c.player_name, trans_state)
            if trans_state['current_transport_state'] == 'PLAYING':
                zone_c.pause()

        for zone in self.zones:
            if zone.is_playing_tv:
                zone.volume = 0
            else:
                zone.volume = 40

        for zone_c in coordinators:
            print('Play doorbell', zone_c.player_name)
            zone_c.play_uri('x-file-cifs://DoorPi/DoorPiPublic/doorbell/sounds/Tbell.mp3')

        time.sleep(14)
        for zone in self.zones:
            if zone.is_playing_tv:
                pass
            else:
                print('restoring {}'.format(zone))
                zone.snap.restore(fade=True)
```

Yes, this is indeed possible. If you already have the SoCo object for your player, you can use `player.volume` to get the volume level. It is a property, which means you can read and assign it like a variable: 
```python
vol = player.volume
player.volume = vol+2
```
Please take a look at the [documentation](https://soco.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/soco.core.html#soco.core.SoCo.volume) for more information.

> Did I missed something obvious here or is this a bug ?

Yes, you're not looking at the [Namespaces in XML](http://www.w3.org/TR/xml-names/) spec, which places far stricter rules on names with colons in them. Almost all XML implementations implement XML *with* Namespaces in XML, and hence one is bound by the stricter rules the latter places on colons.

Also, FWIW, XML 1.1 is mostly abandoned and only really of historical interest. XML 1.0 5th Edition mostly makes it obsolete (for better or for worse, given the changes in 5th ed…), and XML 1.0 4th Edition or XML 1.0 5th Edition are what almost everyone implements (yes, they differ normatively!) — 1.1 never saw such widespread deployment.

97002 ??  R        0:06,39 /usr/local/bin/python2.7 /usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/exabgp/application/bgp.py --folder /usr/local/etc/exabgp /usr/local/etc/exabgp/exabgp.conf

BGP/MPLS are special in this respect: for the purpose of determining whether two routes are about the same NLRI or not, the Label is *not* considered part of the NLRI.

For instance a router can send an Update to change the label used for a said RD:Prefix; this will not be considered a new route by BGP.

In fact conceptually, the Label should be considered part of the nexthop information. But to allow more efficient packing of multiple prefixies in one BGP update in the case of many routes having same BGP attributes (e.g. routes of a same VPN having same Route Targets) but not having the same Label, the Label is encoded in the NLRI field.

Your understanding of RFC3107 is fine, and the fact that RFC2547/4364 are an exception to the rule is not obvious by reading the specs. I'll try to find the reference.

So for both (a) and (b), things should be implemented as if the Label was encoded outside of the NLRI.

Following up here with discussion from the PR:

This issue is actually more like a bug/wart in boto2, which doesn't set the AllowReassociation parameter and doesn't provide any way to explicitly set that parameter to false. Without it, AWS defaults to true, allowing an EIP to be reassociated even if it is currently associated with another instance.

https://github.com/ab/boto/commit/239013bfef349763ac9ef73cc67bba0e0eb4adda is a start at fixing this for boto 2.

`stream` affects the way you will receive the response. If it's set to False, you'll get a single chunk of data once the request completes. If True, it'll be a generator that streams data as it is received.

For most use-cases, people should use `stream=True`, unless you're looking at a very few lines of logs that you expect to get immediately.

If you set `follow=True` and `stream=False`, the method will wait for the request to complete (container exits or is stopped) or until the HTTP timeout is hit, whichever comes first. That combination is not the most useful, but those 2 parameters have separate meanings and they should stay separate.

@cburgdorf 👍

Lemme try to clarify.

We need `get_xdg_trinity_root` for the case where it hasn't been overridden in the command line or via environment variable.

Currently, `trinity.utils.chains.get_local_data_dir` uses `get_xdg_trinity_root` to determine the appropriate data directory.  Since we're allowing the trinity root dir to be set via CLI, we need to do one of two things.

1. When `--trinity-root-dir` has been specified in the command line, we populate the environment variable `XDG_TRINITY_ROOT` with the provided value so that `get_xdg_trinity_root` returns the correct value.
2. Change the call signature to `get_local_data_dir` to instead require that the trinity root dir be explicitely provided.

I'm in favor of option 2 as I think it is less magic.

So, we need to modify all call sites to `get_local_data_dir` to provide the `trinity_root_dir`.  This is probably primarily in the `ChainConfig` object and tests.

Two people reported (three if you include my own test) that it fixed: https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/2687 and I hope it also fixes https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/2697

As far as I can tell, these are race condition in Windows. The Windows CRT is pretty buggy around removing and rename files and then immediately accessing them. It may be shell plugins and virus scanners in play to, but the Windows file deletion rules lead to this situation (on the other OSes files are properly reference counted and deleted when the last reference goes away, on Windows this is not the case).

I don't *think* my change would be the **cause** of any backtraces you saw. Rather, any backtraces will end up in my function now instead of at the location of the original function because all that function does is to retry the operation 5 times with exponential random back-off, and if none of the attempts succeeds then it raises the exception.

I'd also like to add a word of explanation for the 'openssl' special case: The official project files to build Python 2.7 on Windows (see `<python_src>/PCbuild/pcbuild.sln`) compile openssl along with the rest of the Python core in order to allow Python to open 'https:' URLs. For this to work, the openssl sources must be located in a specific directory, and I created a recipe 'openssl-src-for-python' which takes care of this. Package 'openssl-src-for-python' has the same version number as the contained openssl itself, requiring the special case. Fetching packages ...

```
I did a little digging and found that the md5 mismatch is a platform issue. 

According to https://anaconda.org/r/nlopt/files the package linked to https://anaconda.org/r/nlopt/2.4.2/download/osx-64/nlopt-2.4.2-1.tar.bz2 should in fact be 74664eb66b8c58c611c0b4db372b639f, which is on the right side of the inequality in the snippet above.

On the left, 645c580fbff222b93ee51b6378b827a6 is checksum associated with https://anaconda.org/r/nlopt/2.4.2/download/win-32/nlopt-2.4.2-1.tar.bz2
The weird part is that when you actually download the tarball at the osx64 address (above), what is downloaded is the win32 package.

> Academic and research users like me install a lot of packages

Installing a lot of packages into the same environment "just in case" is not a good idea. Packages declare constraints on other packages and when there are a large number of packages this leaves the solver with little 'wriggle room' for the packages you actually need.

Instead you should use conda's comprehensive isolation support provided by conda [environments](https://conda.io/docs/using/envs.html).

@mingwandroid is right in my opinion.  Conda environments, in almost all cases, are incredibly cheap in terms of overhead and weight.  The size limit on PATH is also another factor.  Having separate environments means now you have to maintain many more conda environments.  Packages in every environment now have to be maintained and updated.  Environments are sure to overlap and shadow each other.  You now need to track binaries and libraries in each environment. On top of that, order of activation would be incredibly important.

If using conda environments requires you to be a power-user, I think the subtle issues that could arise from stacking environments would be far beyond a power-user.  Not only would you have to understand environments, but you would have to understand how environments interact with each other.

I'd recommend:
UseTab: ForContinuationAndIndentation
PointerAlignment: Left

Tabs are useful for indentation (at the start of a line), because they look good at whatever width the tab character is configured to. But with tabs anywhere else on the line, there is a danger of messing up formatting if you want to align certain things across lines. For example, the variable names here are properly aligned in VS if tab width = 4, but not in GitHub, where tab width = 8:
https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/src/BulletDynamics/Featherstone/btMultiBody.h#L712

As per Google's style, I'd also make it a recommendation to avoid multiple declarations of pointers on one line. https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html#Pointer_and_Reference_Expressions

If you insist on doing the culling yourself, a contactPairTest could be further optimized to select the most optimal algorithm.

Unfortunately, I'm not aware of the "right" answer here, because the applications I work on use a domain-specific (fixed) set of states. The go-to choice in the frequentist framework is AIC, so I think you could just use that. However, in my experience the linear penalty (-2k) imposed by AIC becomes negligible if the number of samples is large. 

By the way, have you looked into nonparametric extensions of HMMs? There's a Python library implementing [HDP-HMM](http://www.stats.ox.ac.uk/~teh/research/npbayes/TehJor2010a.pdf), called [`pyhsmm`](https://github.com/mattjj/pyhsmm). I haven't used it personally, but it might be worth a try.

Here's what i found slows down my notebook the most -

1. slowness is directly proportional to # of cells in the notebook - does not matter if they are code cells or markdown, merging cells reduces slowness (but export your cells as .py files & import them if your merged cells get too big)

2. typesetting addons - i see more latex & math formatting messages preventing my notebook becoming accessible if the # of cells grows

3. Jupyterlab is faster than Jupyter for the same notebook

4. Starting from a notebook with cleared outputs is faster than starting from an notebook with outputs already inline in the notebook

5. big cells - now i move my big functions to .py files in the same dir & import them

6. Restarting the kernel to re run causes a reload but execution is faster than re run all cells without restarting

7. Use of the nbextensions reduce cell width extension . notebooks where this has not been used for any cells are faster than notebooks with cells that line up as columns

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3260659/40795206-f4e77a72-651e-11e8-9a73-331fc5c208a9.png)

8. errors in your notebook. The error occured on the 2nd last cpu spike, but the notebook was unresponsive until after the last spike - it is not responsive enough to even scroll to the error until the last spike

This is what is see in logs sometimes. When this happens i have to reboot my conda environment & restart my browser to get working again

There are numerous well-documented problems on many filesystems with large numbers of files in a single directory.  (Start [here](http://serverfault.com/questions/43133/filesystem-large-number-of-files-in-a-single-directory), for example.)  The definition of "large" has been getting better over time, but it's still something to consider carefully.  And unless the filesystem is B-tree-based or similar, finding a file in a directory is typically a linear search (i.e., O(n)).  Fortunately, most modern filesystems are tree/hash-based by default (e.g., [ext4's dir_index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ext4)).

It sounds like you're trying an import in two different installations of Python, or two different environments. Check `sys.executable` to see which Python and environment you're running in, and `sys.path` to see where it looks to import modules.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

For more detail, see [this blog post](http://takluyver.github.io/posts/i-cant-import-it.html).

- Update ipykernel to the latest version
- Reinstall the kernelspec ([instructions](http://ipython.readthedocs.io/en/stable/install/kernel_install.html)) using the older ipykernel.

> I don't know schematics internals, but would it make sense to allow providing a custom serializer (per model or per field)? And then bundle implementation for the ones you listed.

Incidentally I'm working on this, too.

The serialization mechanism is already extensible up to a point, but it could be better. Specifically, I want to generalize the export system so that, instead of choosing between `to_native()` and `to_primitive()`, you could specify a mixed representation as well.

Consider:

```python
class Foo(object):
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x, self.y = x, y

class FooType(BaseType):
    def to_native(self, value, context):
        # convert dict to Foo instance
    def to_primitive(self, value, context):
        # convert Foo instance to dict


class M(Model):

    _id = ObjectIdType()
    dt = UTCDateTimeType()
    foo = FooType()
```

Let's say I want to export an `M` instance and feed it to PyMongo. Well, PyMongo requires an actual `ObjectId` instance but wouldn't understand a native `Foo` instance. So ideally I'd like to export

  * the model container itself as primitive (`dict`)
  * `_id` as native (an `ObjectId` instance)
  * `dt` as native (a `datetime.datetime` instance)
  * `foo` as primitive (`dict`).

There's currently no simple way to accomplish this.

My current plan is to specify these things in an `ExportProfile` object, which could be extended by applications in any way they want. During serialization, the profile object is consulted about what to do with each field.

Right now, `export_loop` (responsible for serializing models) does take a `field_converter` function that can already be customized if you want to intercept a converted value and do something with it. But this mechanism has two shortcomings: it doesn't let you drop values altogether, and the `field_converter` is not invoked for compound types. I intend to fix the latter issue by unifying the interfaces so that the same `field_converter` can be applied to all fields. The first limitation is something the `ExportProfile` objects could eventually address.

> is there another ticket somewhere?

Well, the issue has been raised several times, and #249 is being kept open.

#263 is a RFC on the feature. See [this comment](https://github.com/schematics/schematics/issues/263#issuecomment-150047136) for a summary of the current alternatives for the internal implementation.

It's more about the version of python and some of the SSL library packages that came with 2.7.
ssl_verify is either False, or a path to the SSL key chain.

PuppetDB runs on 2 ports 8080 on localhost and 8081 on all interfaces.  If you are on the local machine you use the 8080 URL and you do not need any sort of SSL Key.  If you are remove you will need to use 8081 for SSL and you will have to provide the puppetdb SSL key with curl.

Sounds like when you are using pypuppet you are using 8081 and your curl is using 8080.

Those docs on readthedocs.org are old, I didn't even knew the existed.  I guess I need to figure out how to update those docs.  There are changes to python and how it manages SSL key trusts.

Your best bet is to not put the puppetdb key in your SSL keychain and just keep it somewhere with your code so that you can reference it with ssl_verify='/path to ssl'  unless you are doing everything local.

Sounds good to me. PyCharm seems to be good about exposing stdout during test runs, at least based on the fact that `print` works without issue.

If the tests are failing then the full stdout should be included in the HTML report. If they're passing you may need to run pytest with `-rpP` to include this in the report. If you mean that you'd like to add this extra data as an asset in the report, the following snippet modified from the one in the README should help. You'll need to stash `log` somewhere so that it can be accessed from the `pytest_runtest_makereport` hook:

```python
import pytest
@pytest.mark.hookwrapper
def pytest_runtest_makereport(item, call):
    pytest_html = item.config.pluginmanager.getplugin('html')
    outcome = yield
    report = outcome.get_result()
    extra = getattr(report, 'extra', [])
    if report.when == 'call':
        xfail = hasattr(report, 'wasxfail')
        if (report.skipped and xfail) or (report.failed and not xfail):
            # add custom log to report on failure
            extra.append(pytest_html.extras.test(log))
        report.extra = extra
```

@leecow, this still isn't as "simple" as I (or some others) would probably like it because, for official RTM or Preview releases, this still requires parsing some external data file in order to determine what to download.

At this point, the azure blob URL is the closest to what I would think is "ideal". That is, a URL where all I need to replace is the version and it will continue downloading the correct thing. The improvement that could be made, would be that a `https://dot.net` URL be provided the forwards to the azure blob URL.

NuGet is a perfect example of "easy to use" and "easy to update". They provide a single sub-domain on their site for all downloads (`https://dist.nuget.org`) and easy to parse URLs.

If I want the latest NuGet, I can always grab from: https://dist.nuget.org/win-x86-commandline/latest/nuget.exe
If I want a specific version, I can always grab from: https://dist.nuget.org/win-x86-commandline/v{version}/nuget.exe (example: v4.3.0 is https://dist.nuget.org/win-x86-commandline/v4.3.0/nuget.exe).

This allows me to write scripts where I can have a string containing the basic URL format and replace just the version and expect it to continue working.

```
# Top of file, all defaults for variable inputs
$nuget_version = "2.0.0"

# Code

$nuget_url = "https://dist.nuget.org/win-x86-commandline/v$nuget_version/nuget.exe"
DownloadFile($nuget_url)

# More Code
```

Now, when a new version of NuGet comes out, I replace a few characters at the top of my script and I'm done. I don't have to download any intermediate files, I don't have to parse the data, I don't have to have a dependency on JSON.NET, I don't have to use anything that isn't basic Bash or Powershell commands, etc.

Yes, Chrome and Internet Explorer share the same certificate technology and also access to sites via SNI, so both fail on XP in either case. Firefox has its own support for both, so Firefox works on XP in most cases the others don't.

HOWEVER, people who are stuck on XP are often there because they have few other choices. I don't think Chrome is so much the worry here, but Internet Explorer 8 users. Despite lack of support for XP and IE8, it is going to be around for a long time to come, and they are being locked out. Often the people with this kit are either forced to use a particular configuration by employers, possibly because they use old software which is essential to their business but won't work on newer OS or they won't invest in new hardware, or, more seriously in my opinion, because they are among the most disadvantaged, often in poor countries, and least knowledgeable people - they can't afford to upgrade, and they haven't a clue why things don't work any more. Or it is older people who find change very difficult - they have understood their computer, just about, over a long period, and they can't face the prospect of change, even quite minor change.

One of the really pernicious things about this problem is that there is no opportunity to say what's gone wrong and to offer advice (e.g. "use Firefox, it's got a longer shelf life on your hardware"), and worse, what does happen is extremely frightening for these users - it's all about how someone is attacking you (same is true of SNI on XP/IE8).

So I think we do the people least able to sort out their situation a huge disservice by not addressing this issue with some importance. There's an equality and fairness problem behind the technical one.

>I think part of the problem is that timelex seems to actually just be a tokenizer, not a proper lexer (e.g. a tokenizer that attaches some additional context to the tokens). 

Sorry, looking at `timelex` again, I'm wrong, it does in fact maintain some additional state / context about the tokens. As I mentioned earlier, I'm not familiar enough with the theory of parsing to know if this is the right level of abstraction, but I suspect that it *may* be close to it - most of what we care about, context-wise, is in the semantic meaning of the tokens, which I think would normally done as part of the construction of an abstract syntax tree (though we're not parsing a compiler with a well-defined grammar, so I'm not sure how well the analogy to that sort of parser holds).

@ksonj I'll double check the documentation, but this is basically the expected behavior. The default parser behavior is that if an element is missing, it is replaced with the corresponding element from the `default` parameter. The `default` parameter is optional, and defaults to a timezone-naive `datetime` object representing midnight today's date.

Since an empty string contains no date elements, all elements are replaced with the values from the default element. The only time `ValueError` is raised is if the date string is malformed in some way.

In 2.5.0 (and in the current experimental version in the master branch here), I've added a `smart_defaults` option which tweaks this behavior somewhat, but would still parse an empty string as a valid date.

I think that it might be a good idea to change this in 2.5.0, though. A date string containing no date likely qualifies as exceptional. I would think it's also particularly important when doing fuzzy date parsing, since there's no general and obvious way to tell whether the date returned came from the string or from the default, e.g.:

```python
    >>> from dateutil.parser import parse
    >>> from datetime import datetime

    >>> dflt_date = datetime(2015, 03, 03)

    >>> parse('What a lovely day it was.', fuzzy=True, default=dflt_date)
    datetime.datetime(2015, 3, 3, 0, 0)

    >>> parse('What a lovely day it was on March 3rd.', fuzzy=True, default=dflt_date)
    datetime.datetime(2015, 3, 3, 0, 0)
```

@jamesblackburn OK, I've found the problem - March 25, 2007 at 1 AM is not a valid time in `Europe/London`, which is why the semantics are wonky:

```python
from datetime import datetime as dt
from dateutil import tz
LON = tz.gettz('Europe/London')
tz.datetime_exists(datetime(2007, 3, 25, 1, tzinfo=LON)  # False
tz.datetime_exists(datetime(2007, 3, 25, 0, tzinfo=LON)  # True
```
The reason the equality semantics are inconsistent between `y` and `z` is that `y` is treated as an in-zone comparison and the wall clock (midnight vs. 1AM) is compared, whereas when `x` and `z` are compared, it's treated as an inter-zone comparison, and both are converted to UTC, and *that* is compared. For the `y == z` comparison, that works well because neither `y` or `z` are imaginary times, and they indeed both represent the same UTC time - being that they are the same wall time in the same zone.

However, `x` doesn't exist and as such it does not map to *any* `UTC` time, and so when you try to compare it to something in another zone, it is erroneously mapped to the point in UTC that is 1 DST-offset *before* the beginning of the gap - which is the same value that is created when you created `y` and `z` by passing them through a stage where they were represented as UTC.

So, the lessons are:

1. Check to see if the datetimes you construct are imaginary when you create them if you're working in zones with gaps.
2. Use `tzinfo` singletons wherever possible. In this case `y` had what I would consider to be closer to the right answer (though it's not clear that there is a right answer since `x` does not actually represent any real time).

There are two changes glued together in a single commit: moving of some http methods to B2RawApi and a change in how Bucket objects are constructed.

The second change forces the user to explicitly provide an instance of raw_api and account_info when constructing the bucket object. It is a non-small change in the API interface (thank you for keeping it on a branch for discussion).

It used to be
```py
api = B2Api()
bucket_dict = api.something('...')
bucket = BucketFactory.from_api_bucket_dict(api, bucket_dict)
```
and now it will be
```py
api = B2Api()
bucket_dict = api.something('...')
raw_api = B2RawApi()
account_info = StoredAccountInfo()  # wrong! should be account_info = B2Api().account_info
bucket = BucketFactory.from_api_bucket_dict(raw_api, account_info, bucket_dict)
```

The change also isolates the Bucket class from the B2Api class completely, which might be fine, but we might want to use some B2Api methods from something like `Bucket.sync_down()` (for example `B2Api.download_by_id()`). Passing a B2Api instance encapsulated its properties like `account_info`, `raw_api`, `cache` or any other (maybe `b2_session` in the future), which might just be useful when performing all kinds of bucket operations. Therefore I think we should not make this change and keep Bucket having an `api` as a member. At the same time, it is ok to do something like `self.raw_api = api.raw_api` in `Bucket.__init__` for convenience.

As for moving the actual http calls from Bucket to B2RawApi, I wholeheartedly approve.

A few things that come to mind:
1. Are you initializing your `dataArray` at some point? It should be initialized (probably to zeros) before the first calls to `predict()` - so if any of the sensors' readings are missing, you would still have some numerical value in the array.
2. It seems that you're only casting Accelerometer data to `Float`. Are the other sensors guaranteed to be floats? Can you try casting these as well?
2. Better yet - can you please try casting all of them to `Double`? (it seems that your `Measurement` is defined as `Double` anyway). The exported model probably expects `Double`s as input (you can check by double-clicking the `mlmodel` file in Xcode).
3. Could it be that you sometimes call `PerformPrediction()` with only part of the sensors in `dataEntry`? For example with a Gyroscope measurement only, but without new Magnetometer reading? 
If so - the other sensors must be attended to somehow (filled with some default values). 
Otherwise - you keep calling some of the sensors with their previous readings, and in case of the first call - with some uninitialized values.  

Could you please try running a simpler model with just one of the sensors, just to rule out all of the possible errors above?  
Could you also please try using your code (all 3 sensors), on the same devices, with the built-in sensors?  

Last but not least - if none of the above helps, could you please try to record some data from your sensor and send it in some format (csv etc)? I'd like to try to reproduce your problem in order to debug it.

As a community effort, this project depends on contributions from the community. It *is* actively maintained and had a release on Friday for 2.2 which includes many months of contributions from a wide variety of individuals.

I respectfully disagree with your conclusion that it is "**completely** unsuitable for **any** production environment or large data sets" (emphasis added). Aside from being hyperbolic (which I don't think is fruitful for technical conversation), I see this claim as false, knowing that I and many others have deployed DJA in a production setting.

IMO, the problem you are reporting is a limitation that is not unique to DJA. Selecting an appropriate queryset is true for DJA and DRF and Django underneath it. I think this is an area where Django (and Python in general) prefers being explicit over implicit. Rather than trying to intuit the appropriate `selected_related` additions, it is up to the developer to include this in the queryset. This strategy prevents code from over-selecting from other tables and bloating a db query. I thought I demonstrated that as I walked through how to eliminate the N+1 query bug from the example app. If I failed to make that clear enough, I apologize.

The `TransformerLayer` will do bilinear interpolation, though, not max-pooling. You could use it to extract regions scaled to a fixed target size, but not to implement the ROI pooling discussed here.
Looking at [the implementation](https://github.com/rbgirshick/caffe-fast-rcnn/blob/fast-rcnn/src/caffe/layers/roi_pooling_layer.cu), it seems the most efficient solution will indeed be wrapping it into a custom kernel.
Implementing it in pure Theano will probably be slow. You'd need to `theano.scan()` over the region proposals, extract the corresponding subtensor, subdivide that again (to get, e.g., 7x7 subregions) and take the maximum of each.

>  I think (real) fixtures are bad: difficult to maintain, I really prefer factories (from factory boy),

That's a dangerous idea. Real fixtures are often closer to real usage. When they need maintenance, that's often for a good reason and highlights backwards incompatibilities.

Factories and their dependencies are more complex set up and hard to understand the scope of (what cases they are set up to cover). It adds automation, but the cost is the added complexity.

In this particular project, we have the case of money, currencies, custom managers, arithmetics etc.. so the automation probably has to be combined with hard-coded fixtures.

Hi @jorenham,

Which version of `wagtail-modeltranslation` are you using?
I suggest you use the latest - v0.8 and please note that `WagtailTranslationOptions` were removed in v0.8, refer to [Quick Start](https://github.com/infoportugal/wagtail-modeltranslation#quick-start) on how to replace it.

Lastly, note that translating one of Wagtail's own model will result in a Wagtail migration or you'll need to use django-modeltranslation's `sync_translation_fields`. Please read django-modeltranslation's [Committing fields to database](http://django-modeltranslation.readthedocs.io/en/latest/registration.html#committing-fields-to-database) so you understand the implications.

If possible I suggest you subclass `Image` and translate the child class.

I would say that generally you would want to patch out any checksums that deal with file integrity and don't affect functionality after the initial check. As far as I know, the CRC32 check for a PNG file only checks for corrupted bits during file transfer and is not really useful for fuzzing or vulnerability discovery as an attacker could just recalculate their payload's CRC32 sum and replace it. Plus, it slows down the symbolic execution. The LLVM fuzzing project also recommends to patch out the CRC check or replace it with something more friendly: http://llvm.org/docs/LibFuzzer.html#fuzzer-friendly-build-mode If you'd like to keep it in there as an exercise, then I'm afraid I can't give you an exact answer, but here is something similar that might [help](https://yurichev.com/blog/symbolic/#Cyclic%20redundancy%20check). Here are some academic papers that might give a better idea about the efficacy of symbolic execution for hash protocols [[1]](https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/1e06/09bd8fbf12ba1c1565a716421c77d96ceb0a.pdf) [[2]](http://lenx.100871.net/papers/TaintScope-Tissec.pdf). Here is a nice graph from [[1]](https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/1e06/09bd8fbf12ba1c1565a716421c77d96ceb0a.pdf) that shows time to calculate CRC32 with KLEE and STP: [1]: https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/1e06/09bd8fbf12ba1c1565a716421c77d96ceb0a.pdf -- Efficient Symbolic Execution for Analysing Cryptographic Protocol Implementations [2]: http://lenx.100871.net/papers/TaintScope-Tissec.pdf -- Checksum-Aware Fuzzing Combined with Dynamic Taint Analysis and Symbolic Execution

```
# Connect to the Vehicle
print 'Connecting to vehicle on: %s' % args.connect
vehicle = connect(args.connect, baud=57600)
```
to 
```
# Connect to the Vehicle
print 'Connecting to vehicle on: %s' % args.connect
vehicle = connect(args.connect, wait_ready=True, baud=57600)
```

You have also removed this line which is important since it tells you that the vehicle is ready to arm. If you don't have the line then you may be sending the arm command when the vehcle is not ready - and the command will be silently dropped.
```
while not self.vehicle.is_armable:
            time.sleep(.1)
```
If you want to find out *why* it isn't arming, then do something like:

```
if v.mode.name == "INITIALISING":

    time.sleep(1)
while vehicle.gps_0.fix_type < 2:
    print "Waiting for GPS...:", vehicle.gps_0.fix_type
    time.sleep(1)
while not self.vehicle.is_armable:
            time.sleep(.1)
```

IPrepareApp inherits from IApplication and the prepared stuff is in there. Please inherit from that rather than IApplication. It also causes issues if another defunct session holds the lockfile. This is particularly bad if the lxplus connection drops whilst jobs are submitting:
```python
WARNING  Auto-Failing job in bad state: 2622 was submitting
WARNING  To try this job again resubmit or use a backend.reset()
INFO     Forcing job 2622 to status "failed"
RegistryLockError: Could not lock 'jobs' object #2622! Object is locked by session 'lxplus028.cern.ch.15.36_Friday_06_April_2018.PID.30748 (pid jobs) since PID.30748' 
(consider --debug option for more information)

```

I do think it's useful to have at least the `INFO`, `WARNING`, `ERROR` parts to make it clearer to the user when we really want their attention. I don't think we want the function name part (`submit`) as that's very much an implementation detail. As for the module name (`Job`) I'm not sure how useful it is to the user but I do think it helps give some context for the more ambiguous messages.

I think the best approach is just to get in the habit for us developers of using a custom verbose string for our own work and not flooding the users with info which is only useful for us.

Now that we are here I think we have a few problems with the current way we handle preconditions and tombstones:

- We use `deleted` for precondition checks, but if a record exists we can change the `deleted` field to true and it will behave as a deleted record.
- Before #1102 we used to allow checking tombstones with `If-Match` checks but we also allowed creation with `If-None-Match: *`. Now tombstones behave like non-existing records. In my opinion tombstones should always have the same behavior of records for precondition checks.

Thanks for raising this issue.  The discrete wavelet transform routines currently also use that `_check_dtype` utility and the C code for convolution does not currently support complex data types.  The `cwt` routine does not have this problem as it uses `np.convolve` which supports complex data types.  I think a reasonable solution would be to just make a `_check_dtype_cwt` that has the behavior you describe and leave the `_check_dtype` cython function as is for now.  

Are you interested in making a PR for this?  I don't think it would even be necessary to have `_check_dtype_cwt` be in a Cython file.  It could just be a function within `cwt.py` itself.  

That said, in PR #300 I did add C99 complex support to the C-code convolution routines and the complex types you mention are included within `_check_dtype` as part of that PR.  That PR is mainly concerned with the discrete transform (to remove the need for calling the transforms on the real and imaginary parts of the data independently as in the lines [here](https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/52b1808c2353f1b78433e03e36301b63d3037749/pywt/_dwt.py#L150-L154)).  That PR has been stalled for a bit pending a decision on how best to handle detection of C99 support (which is lacking in MSVC), but if merged would also resolve the issue raised in this PR.

Hi @jjoachim 
The requirement is completely reasonable, and we are looking into ways to enable this. 

At the moment the frame sub-system is trying to:
1. Provide thread-safe API for the user to manage the lifetime of individual frames 
2. Ensure fast and (more importantly) deterministic frame allocations when streaming

To accomplish both we have (a somewhat over-complicated) buffer-pool mechanism. That's why there is no API to create one single frame at the moment. 

One way to deal with this is to define a custom `processing_block`:
```cpp
processing_block pb([&](rs2::frame f, const rs2::frame_source& source)
{
    // source can be used to allocate new frames and send them out
});
frame_queue q;
pb.start(q); // Results from the block will be enqueued into q
pb.invoke(input_frame); // Invoke the lambda above
frame output_frame = q.wait_for_frame(); // Fetch the result
```
In this case, new frames will be allocated from the buffer-pool that exists inside the `processing_block` object. 
Unfortunately, this API is not well documented yet and isn't very user-friendly either. 

In addition, I am currently working on a "software only" device that can be used to generate frames from arbitrary data. It is still a [work-in-progress](https://github.com/dorodnic/librealsense/tree/bypass_dev), but will be most likely merged next week. The plan is to use such device to better unit-test point-cloud, sync and align methods, but one could also use it to inject data from an external source. Keeping your use case in mind, I will try to add a decent code-sample alongside this change.

Regarding spelling - the team is diverse and contains non-native English speakers (myself included). Fortunately, none of the misspellings are on the binary API boundary (C API), so we will fix it ASAP.
Thanks for pointing this out.

Hi @svensht2 
Regarding your question about compiler support: 
You are correct to point out that we strive to move forward with the language. We are not trying to chase after the latest and greatest in language support, but rather targeting what can be considered a reasonable modern build system at the moment. Both GCC5 and Visual Studio 2015 are very common right now, so we are planning to stick to them until there is good enough reason to upgrade. (Unfortunately, VS13 was suffering from multiple compiler bugs when dealing with C++11 code. As for GCC, if I recall correctly the reason for the jump was a requirement to integrate JSON support)  
In addition, we offer binary versions of the SDK that can be used with Visual Studio 2010+ and GCC 4, and with some minor modifications (switching to `boost::shared_ptr`) even earlier build systems.

`wait_for_frames()` will block the current thread until new frames become available.
from my understanding `poll_for_frames()` ask if there is a frame ready without blocking the thread, and it will retrieve the latest undelivered frameset. 

Documentation for `poll_for_frames()`

> Check if a new set of frames is available and retrieve the latest undelivered set. The frames set includes time-synchronized frames of each enabled stream in the pipeline. The method returns without blocking the calling thread, with status of new frames available or not. If available, it fetches the latest frames set. Device frames, which were produced while the function wasn't called, are dropped. 
> To avoid frame drops, this method should be called as fast as the device frame rate. The application can maintain the frames handles to defer processing. However, if the application maintains too long history, the device may lack memory resources to produce new frames, and the following calls to this method shall return no new frames, until resources are retained.

Documentation for `wait_for_frames()`

> Wait until a new set of frames becomes available. The frames set includes time-synchronized frames of each enabled stream in the pipeline. In case of different frame rates of the streams, the frames set include a matching frame of the slow stream, which may have been included in previous frames set. The method blocks the calling thread, and fetches the latest unread frames set. Device frames, which were produced while the function wasn't called, are dropped. To avoid frame drops, this method should be called as fast as the device frame rate. The application can maintain the frames handles to defer processing. However, if the application maintains too long history, the device may lack memory resources to produce new frames, and the following call to this method shall fail to retrieve new frames, until resources are retained.

I tried running this script, and had to make some minor modifications for it to succeed. Here's a diff:

```diff
diff --git a/Makefile b/Makefile
index e20d51c..b21d4be 100644
--- a/Makefile
+++ b/Makefile
@@ -4,6 +4,7 @@ CPU_COUNT=$(shell python -c "from multiprocessing import cpu_count; print(cpu_co
 help:
 	@echo "Please use \`make <target>' where <target> is one of:"
 	@echo "  help           to show this message"
+	@echo "  publish        to upload dist/* to PyPi"
 	@echo "  all            to to execute all following targets (except \`test')"
 	@echo "  docs-html      to generate HTML documentation"
 	@echo "  docs-clean     to remove documentation"
@@ -62,7 +63,7 @@ package:
 package-clean:
 	rm -rf build dist pulp_smash.egg-info
 
-publish:
+publish: package
 	twine upload dist/*
 
 .PHONY: help all docs-html docs-clean lint-flake8 lint-pylint lint test \
diff --git a/release.sh b/release.sh
index 2195082..f12441f 100755
--- a/release.sh
+++ b/release.sh
@@ -24,17 +24,20 @@ make package-clean package
 for python in python{2,3}; do
     venv="$(mktemp --directory)"
     virtualenv -p "${python}" "${venv}"
+    set +u
     source "${venv}/bin/activate"
+    set -u
     for dist in dist/*; do
-        pip install "${dist}"
-        python -m pulp_smash
-        pip uninstall -y pulp_smash
+        pip install --quiet "${dist}"
+        python -m pulp_smash 1>/dev/null
+        pip uninstall --quiet --yes pulp_smash
     done
     deactivate
     rm -rf "${venv}"
 done
 
 # Get the changes from last release and commit
+git add VERSION
 git commit -m "Release version ${NEW_VERSION}" \
     -m "Shortlog of commits since last release:" \
     -m "$(git shortlog ${OLD_VERSION}.. | sed 's/^./    &/')"
```

The new `--quiet` and `1>/dev/null` bits are there just to keep the script output to a reasonably small level. The locations of the virtualenvs are still printed, so if the script aborts, the user can still find and destroy what's been created in `/tmp/`.

Bilen, I think I cracked it.  By the way, Guido wanted the `pep8` script to be renamed to `pycodestyle`
Step 1: `pip install pycodestyle`

Step 2: in .git/hooks, create a file with executable permissions thus:
  `#!/bin/sh` 
  `pycodestyle --first --exclude=bin,lib,include,sampledata`


Step 3:  try a commit.  It will be aborted if any bad stuff is found, because pycodestyle returns non-zero exit status if it finds anything.

I don't think graphene should be responsible for defining the default timezone for a datetime since it could misrepresent the actual timezone for the underlying value. If the datetime value is timezone aware then it will be represented in the response accurately. If the datetime value is not timezone aware then we can't assume it is in UTC.

It is the responsibility of the developer to ensure that timezone aware datetimes are resolved for any `DateTime` types and not the framework.

Avoid to add `conan remote add` is very good, however, if I using some VM for all builds and it has my conan remote address, so conan_package_tools will conflict with this name.  
My opinion is, when CONAN_UPLOAD is present, before to set `upload_repo`, the script might scan if the address already exist in `conan remote list`. If yes, then, use that remote instead to create `upload_repo`. 

e.g.

```python
    upload = os.getenv("CONAN_UPLOAD", False)
    if upload:
        upload_remote_name = False
        # conan.remotes = {"conan.io": "http://server.conan.io", "bintray": "https://api.bintray.com/conan"}
        for repo_name, repo_address in conan.remotes.items():
            # CONAN_UPLOAD is in conan remote list
            if  repo_address == upload:
                # reuse the same remote name
                upload_remote_name = repo_name
                break
         if not upload_remote_name:
             # CONAN_UPLOAD should be add in remote list
             upload_remote_name = "upload_repo"
             conan.remotes[upload_remote_name] = upload
```

The same should be applied for `CONAN_REMOTES`, to avoid errors from "ERROR: Remote 'foobar' already exists with same URL."

You can fix item 1 above (and hence resolve this issue) by updating `setuptools` to the latest version before running `setup.py`.  [`setuptools` 36.2.0 fixed a bug](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/history.html#v36-2-0) that's generating invalid requirements metadata in your wheel.

Here's an example illustrating the problem:

```
Example project

% cat > setup.py << 'EOF'
import setuptools

setuptools.setup(
    name='foo',
    version='1.0.0',
    install_requires=[
        'futures >= 3.1.1;python_version < "3.2"',
    ],
)
EOF


With broken setuptools

% rm -fr foo
% /opt/rh/rh-python36/root/usr/bin/python -m venv foo
% source foo/bin/activate
% python --version
% pip install --upgrade pip setuptools==36.1.1 wheel
% pip freeze --all
pip==9.0.1
setuptools==36.1.1
wheel==0.30.0
% rm -fr build dist foo-1.0.0.dist-info foo.egg-info
% python setup.py bdist_wheel
% deactivate
% unzip dist/foo-1.0.0-py3-none-any.whl
% grep -e '^Requires-Dist:' foo-1.0.0.dist-info/METADATA
Requires-Dist: futures (>=3.1.1)


With fixed setuptools

% rm -fr foo
% /opt/rh/rh-python36/root/usr/bin/python -m venv foo
% source foo/bin/activate
% python --version
% pip install --upgrade pip setuptools==36.2.0 wheel
% pip freeze --all
pip==9.0.1
setuptools==36.2.0
wheel==0.30.0
% rm -fr build dist foo-1.0.0.dist-info foo.egg-info
% python setup.py bdist_wheel
% deactivate
% unzip dist/foo-1.0.0-py3-none-any.whl
% grep -e '^Requires-Dist:' foo-1.0.0.dist-info/METADATA
Requires-Dist: futures (>=3.1.1); python_version < "3.2"
```

You'll also want to adjust the conditions for requiring `futures`, since `futures > 3.1.1` isn't compatible with Python 3 at all.

I think it could be easily done if we follow the Thread Specification and have sort of "link set" and "route set" without maintaining Route TLV from all routers. What i mean is, instead of having `mNextHop `and `mRouteCost `in Router object, we could have sth. like , `mNextHop`, `mNextHopCost`, `mDirectCost`. Then we never set mNextHop to our Router ID, but we keep Router ID of a router that advertised his connection to this router (one with the smallest route cost).

In case of scenario presented by Hubert, when we lose connection to Node C, we will set `mDirectCost `to infinity, and we start using `mNextHop ` (Set to Node B). Also when we do forwarding we need to check if `mNextHopCost` is smaller than `mDirectCost `in order to use `mNextHop` field, otherwise we send frame directly.

Is this feature

(a) an abstraction on top of paging, where each element of the response contains a fixed number of instances of an API resource, and where each element may not correspond to a single request?

(b) a way to hide paging concepts like page_token, next_page_token, and page_size, while still exposing the contents of the page itself?

The examples you gave seem more like (a), but I don't see the benefit over the current version of page streaming that we have. (b) seems like a nice way not to hide the API requests under a Python generator like we currently do. It also doesn't require us to define additional concepts like `batch_size`, since we're just exposing what API already makes available.

In (b), I mean something like:

```
>>> api = PublisherApi()
>>> topic, batch_size = api.topic_path('google.com:pubsub-demo', 'my-topic'), 2
>>> pages = list(api.list_topic_subscriptions(topic, page_size=4)):
>>> pages
[(‘sub_1’, ‘sub_2’, 'sub_3'), (‘sub_4’, ‘sub_5', 'sub_6', 'sub_7'), ('sub_8', )]   
```

where each tuple corresponds to exactly one API response, and contains a maximum of, but not necessarily exactly, four elements, depending on what the response was.

Hi @mrocklin , here are some thoughts:
1. I would say that simply using joblib + dask is good enough when fitting every model takes equal amount of time. In my experience it is always true - for example, if I train a `MLPClassifier` model, then depending on number of neurons selected, the computations will take different amount of time. That would mean that some threads will stay idle, until all computations in batch are finished. If the differences in computations are exponential, which happens sometimes in my projects, this means a lot of idle time.
2. I do agree that approach like the one you suggested would work quite well. Essentially evaluation of `_fit_and_score` function, as is done [here currently in a batch](https://github.com/scikit-optimize/scikit-optimize/blob/1a429b36dc0b59a916720a962bf482fec79ea134/skopt/searchcv.py#L390), would need to be done asynchronously. This could be done by replacing [this line](https://github.com/scikit-optimize/scikit-optimize/blob/1a429b36dc0b59a916720a962bf482fec79ea134/skopt/searchcv.py#L657) with a code which starts `n_jobs` computations in parallel, and waits for one of jobs to finish. As soon as the job is finished, the result of computation should be "told" to the instance of optimization algorithm given in `optimizer`, and then one needs to call `ask` in order to obtain a new point to evaluate. Also all the variables that store evaluation history in `BayesSearchCV` should be updated. 
3. I guess some larger dataset used with your code would be more telling - maybe MNIST?
4. The overhead that you see for `_tell` is where the estimator which models the cross - validation score w.r.t. different parameters is updated. It might take some time, especially after a larger number of iterations are done. This overhead will always happen as when new points are added to the parameter optimizer algorithm.

The extra `make && ` is not the primary reason for the different name. The real issue is what @jagerman mentioned: `test` is a reserved target name which can only be used with CTest.

CTest is not well suited for running Python tests and we really don't want to reinvent Python testing infrastructure in CTest, hence using off-the-shelf pytest. The only way to use the `test` name would be to set up CTest (`enable_testing()`, `add_test()`) with a single test which calls `pytest`. This is pretty bad in a lot of ways. The most obvious is that there would be this huge CTest infrastructure as middleware between `make test` and just a single simple call to `pytest`. But the real showstopper is that CTest has its own way of handling output which mangles pytest's very nice reporting. Then you have to fight with CTest in the middle... or just call `pytest` directly and everything's perfect.

#### Current state of the code

* Extracted the version from common.h and stored in variable ${pybind11_VERSION}

* Collected [3.0.0 release notes](https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.0/release/3.0.0.html#commands) evidence that `add_library(... INTERFACE` started then and adjusted `if`s accordingly.
* With the FindPythonLibsNew in place, I _could_ run Py detection and move [11/14 std](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/CMakeLists.txt#L31-L45) into its own file so that it could be installed, and then actually attach the Py headers and c++ std compile def to the imported target. But that would require real changes to the main CMakeLists.txt . Similarly, I could make the `pybind11_add_module` function useable from an external target. But that, too, means taking it out of the main CMakeLists.txt (or duplicating code). So let me know if you'd actually prefer a dismemberment of the main CMakeLists.txt
* I'm afraid I don't understand pytest, or at least how pytest should imitate a bash script. The requested test is complicated by depending on an installed pybind11. #### Comments on this morning's comments

* It sounds like you'd really like the `pybind11_add_module` functionality available from pybind11Config.cmake. As mentioned above, I believe this means extracting parts of the main CMakeLists.txt into separate files that can be installed (or duplicating code). Does this sound right and acceptable?

* If yes on the above, I'll have a go at extracting `pybind11_add_module` and making it flexible enough for our needs. But even so, it might be better to separate the attributes of the pybind header library and the recommended attributes of the python module built with pybind. That is, continue to have an interface library target (with essentially the python headers and cxx standard properties) and use that library target in `pybind11_add_module` as a link library as well as applying the LTO and binary stripping attributes to the python module.

Fixes #4153.

1) You cannot manually use tls-sni-01 since it requires certbot to send a specific certificate response to a port 443 request, as opposed to just serving a file.
2) We assume that you have a working apache or nginx config. Which means the reader should check the validity of their config before using certbot (if they have made a new one and haven't been already using it to host a webserver). I don't think we assume much else. Previously we assumed that all vhost blocks were in separate files, but now we support more than one vhost per file.
3) The user should use certonly and manually install the certificate - there's lots of information on how to do that online so it shouldn't be too hard. Luckily installation only needs to happen once, and renewal will work even if the initial installation failed (as long as they manually add it to their config files)

I personally didn't know that `.example.org` was shorthand in Nginx for `example.org *.example.org`. Thanks for the link.

Luckily, Certbot understands this though. If you have `.example.org` in your configuration file and provide `--domains example.org,www.example.org,subdomain.example.org` on the command line, Certbot will properly obtain and install your certificate.

We could suggest a certificate for the base of the wildcard and the www subdomain, but I'm not sure we want to do this. If that server block is being used for any other subdomains, users will get certificate errors because their domain isn't contained in the certificate being used by Nginx. Certbot can't possibly know whether or not this is the case and we don't want to suggest actions to the user that will misconfigure their webserver.

Certbot is and should be able to do this if specifically requested though. We could potentially allow users to add their own domains in addition to the domains suggested by Certbot though rather than making them provide these values on the command line.

So, about the python compatibility matrix. I will not make compatible certbot with python 2.7. It will require a least python 3.2.

On 2.x, I have 40 tests that are failing just because of the lack of support for windows file manipulation, in particular the symbolic links. Adding the support manually seems to be quite impossible, and some proposed patches never reached upstream, as basically python 2.x does not receive developments anymore.

And as we can consider certbot on windows as a "new" software, we can follow most of the advices in the community about dropping support for python 2.x.

So here it is. For Linux, where Debian still uses a well supported python 2.x version by default, it seems reasonable to ensure the compatibility. But on Windows, python 2.x is quite a mess, and users should be required to run python 3.2 to run correctly certbot.

I am preparing the CI for Windows accordingly.

1. This is what we currently use to perform dynamic batching including vs not including padding in the size:
https://github.com/salesforce/nonauto-nmt/blob/efcbe4f2329b140ac3ce06abb6409457cebc8e49/run.py#L346-L357
It's worse than yours, because it assumes that the maximum of the padded size of the source and the padded size of the target is a good proxy for the average of the two (instead it's a fairly loose upper bound). I suppose what we should do is provide a generic state-based API:
```python
def batch_size_fn(new, state=None)
    if state is None: state = <initial state>
    state = <update state from new>
    size_proxy = <function of state returning numeric value>
    return size_proxy, state
```
and let the user decide what that state should represent.

I was thinking about a slight different approach on the issue. For each task of an actor, its dependencies come from two parts: its arguments and its previous task. In this problem, there is a chain of uncommitted previous tasks from the same actor, which makes it very time consuming. When forwarding a task for an actor, maybe we could just forward the current task and its arguments, since we know its previous tasks have already been forwarded. It would probably require less  code changes, the downside is it would only apply to actors, while your approach is more generic.

Looks good to me. The virtue is that it handles dependency in batch level. I have some comments:
- For flag `dependency`, `clean` should implies that no other `active` batch depends on this batch. Otherwise if two batches mutually depends on each other, they would reach a deadlock.
- It is hard to checkpoint every Actor states on the batch boundary at a certain moment. When a batch is done, some of the modified Actor may have executed several tasks of other batches, and their current states are not on the batch boundary.

I’m wondering if “distributed actors” is the right name for the feature. This feature essentially implements actor [RMI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Remote_method_invocation) (remote method invocation). The term distributed actors rather implies that actors themselves are distributed, whereas the intended semantics is that the actor handles can be passed around and actor methods can be invoked remotely via those handles. Would actor RMI be a better term for this?

@beardypig the twitch plugin explicitly disables certificate verification:

https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/master/src/streamlink/plugins/twitch.py#L207
```
# The certificate used by Twitch cannot be verified on some OpenSSL versions.
res = http.get(url, params=params, verify=False, headers=headers)
```

changing to `verify=True` removes the warnings.

what's interesting is streamlink should be silencing these InsecureRequestWarnings:

https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/master/src/streamlink/plugin/api/http_session.py#L20
```
# We tell urllib3 to disable warnings about unverified HTTPS requests,
# because in some plugins we have to do unverified requests intentionally.
urllib3.disable_warnings(urllib3.exceptions.InsecureRequestWarning)
```

so the crux of this issue is that call to disable_warnings is no longer working.  if i remove the `try`/`except` block it works, so it isn't throwing an exception.

i can still suppress the warning by setting the `PYTHONWARNINGS` environmental variable, so it's possible:
```
PYTHONWARNINGS="ignore:Unverified HTTPS request" streamlink https://www.twitch.tv/sovietwomble
```

@Ron7TAU, using a column model without a boundary layer almost always will crash. The boundary layer is required to keep the model stable by diffusing heat from the lower layers upwards.

Apart from this, I recommend a few change to your code:
* It is reasonable to assume CO2 is well mixed. You can simply use a constant value of CO2 to begin with.
* If you want a constant surface temperature, the best way is to simply remove the slab surface component from the model. Then, the model does not have `surface_temperature` as a prognostic variable, and so it will remain fixed.
* Use a larger value of `eastward_wind` so that equilibrium is reached faster.
* Use lesser vertical levels so that equilibrium is reached faster. Of course, this is a choice that is set by the scientific objective, not convenience.

I have modified your script, and this 
https://gist.github.com/JoyMonteiro/85038e1590cc7f06f69d0f393a92d4fe
seems to run without crashing. You can modify it to suit your requirements.

You can change CO2 like the following:
```python
state['mole_fraction_of_carbon_dioxide_in_air'].values[:] = 0.00033
```
for 330 ppm, for example. Similarly, you can modify SST:
```python
state['surface_temperature'].values[:] = 295.
```

Remember that this value of `surface_temperature` will be a constant throughout the
simulation.

on the contrary, if someone runs the dycore and wants to analyse the output, it does not make sense that this information is not available easily. It _has_ to be available easily, but I'm open about how it is made available.

> On second thought, I don't think this is the correct decision. In an MPI scenario, this would mean that
there is communication needed to run components which require a whole column.

I don't think this is true. The processors can still be split along latitude/longitude indices, even if those are the fastest-varying indices.

> Even in our current scenario, this would mean array reshaping for every component other the GFS,
which is again inefficient.

This is definitely not true. For the Fortran-wrapped components, the Fortran drivers pretty much all (or actually all) contained ways to specify number of latitudes and longitudes, so that they could operate on arrays whose slowest-varying index is the vertical (what the GFS core wants). Currently there is no array reshaping that occurs for those components.

With the pure Python components, they also generally won't require array reshaping because we've written them to use the same ordering of dims that GFS uses. However, even if they were to use reshaping, for something like a transpose there is no data copying that takes place. The pure Python components will take advantage of numpy array strides, so no copying is necessary to change the dimension ordering in this way.

I propose we keep the current ordering unless we're absolutely sure we need to change it, which I'm not convinced of, based on the above.

However, users that download `chainside-btcpy` from pip will implicitly run `setup.py` to download dependencies, where there is no version pinning. One of the main issues to overcome in order to do what you want to do, is that any "fetch value" request sent to the frontend is going to be asynchronous. There is also the issue that there can be multiple frontends, which would each reply to such a request.

If you have asynchronous code set up, you can have a python function that sends a custom message to the frontend, which causes it to set the model value (which will then be synced to the backend in the regular manner). I think I want to see what the to-be-decided useful methods are before forming an opinion :-).

If it's literally just `sleep` and `current_time`, then ... meh, I don't think it's worth it.

And, I don't think this library should be opinionated, because it's a piece of core functionality that everyone *has* to use. Any methods we put in here become part of the API that every async library has to implement in order to be sniffable. So opinionated stuff should go into downstream libraries like `multio`, so then people can argue about them and choose their favorite.

So my question is... *is* there actually anything beyond `sleep` and `current_time` that everyone agrees on?

First things first, maybe you want something else but:
> How can the new studies check the data they are analyzing? how this information is logged by the orchestration tool (mordred in this case) ? I would work on these two questions first.

If you look for the log line I pasted above, that's the summary after finishing the study and tells you everything the study knows about what was done. It can be improved, can be misleading, or even buggy, but the code for that line comes from:
https://github.com/chaoss/grimoirelab-elk/blob/master/grimoire_elk/enriched/ceres_base.py#L107

So **you can be pretty sure it is written by all new studies** as they are inheriting this method. Of course one could overwrite this method and forget this line, but that could be easily detected as you never get the line you are looking for.

About my proposal, I think we are closer than one might think at first glance:

> @alpgarcia I think your idea would improve the tool, but I would drop those results to the log and use ELK to send them to a ElasticSearch database. In any case your aim is wider than the check we are discussing here.

I proposed a service for logging information and then let this service deciding where to write that information down, so no matter about how persistence is implemented in the data layer used by the service. The important thing (to me), is this service would grant that all people in town use the same format for logging. And I think a simple but functional first implementation of this service would be easy (it could be a web service or not, just a service imported from a common module).

Just think about one single point to control all log messages related to task execution in a common format :D

            self.constants.append(constant)
```

I managed to get my test working by adding a alternative get_worker method that uses SimpleWorker from rq. Maybe this could be added to workers.py as a method? I'll submit a PR if that sounds like a good idea

```
def get_test_worker(*queue_names, **kwargs):
    """
    Returns a RQ worker for all queues or specified ones.
    """
    queues = get_queues(*queue_names)
    name = kwargs.get('name')
    return SimpleWorker(queues, connection=queues[0].connection, name=name)
```
